event: Meeting of Rebekah and Eliezer at the Well
symbols: Rebekah elevated on a camel or platform — the chosen bride, exalted by divine selection
Eliezer kneeling or bowing — the faithful servant fulfilling his master Abraham's commission
gathering of attendants — the household witnesses to God's providential arrangement
well or water source implied — the meeting place where God's guidance was confirmed
lush landscape — the promised land and God's abundant provision for his people
description: A richly colored scene showing a large gathering of figures in an outdoor setting with trees and distant mountains. A young woman in white garments is elevated above the crowd, possibly seated on a camel or elevated platform. Below her, a richly dressed elderly man in red and gold robes gestures toward her in greeting or reverence. Surrounding them are numerous attendants, servants, and onlookers in colorful Eastern garments. The scene is festive and bright, with a lush green landscape behind.
theological_description: This painting depicts the pivotal moment when Abraham's servant Eliezer meets Rebekah at the well (GEN.24). Abraham, now old, sent his trusted servant to find a wife for Isaac from among his kinsmen in Mesopotamia, making him swear not to take a Canaanite bride. Eliezer prayed for a specific sign at the well: 'Let the young woman who says, Let me give your camels a drink also, be the one you have appointed for your servant Isaac' (GEN.24.14). Rebekah fulfilled the sign precisely, demonstrating both her generous character and God's sovereign guidance. The Church Fathers read this narrative typologically: Eliezer represents the Holy Spirit or the apostolic ministry sent to gather a bride (the Church) for the Son (Christ). The well is a recurring biblical symbol of divine encounter (cf. Jesus and the Samaritan woman, JHN.4). Rebekah's willing consent — 'I will go' (GEN.24.58) — prefigures Mary's fiat and the Church's faithful response to the divine call. The entire episode demonstrates that marriage in the patriarchal narratives is not merely social arrangement but divine covenant-making.

scripture_refs.reason: Eliezer's journey and meeting with Rebekah at the well, the scene depicted
Eliezer's prayer for a sign to identify the chosen bride for Isaac
Rebekah's willing consent to go, prefiguring the Church's response to Christ
Isaac takes Rebekah as wife and is comforted after his mother Sarah's death
Jesus meeting the Samaritan woman at the well, the New Testament parallel to well encounters
Christ and the Church as bridegroom and bride, prefigured in Isaac and Rebekah
